$comment: Mock WordPress REST API responses. Each top-level key mirrors a /wp-json/wp/v2/{collection} endpoint. The example's scripts/build.ts wires a custom fetch impl that serves these arrays — so there's no live WordPress server in the loop. Drop a real export here (or point baseUrl at a real site) to swap.

excerpt.rendered: An introduction to publishing your WordPress content as an Agent Content Tree.

content.rendered:
If you're reading this, you found a WordPress blog that publishes its content twice: once as the HTML you're looking at, and once as ACT (Agent Content Tree) — a structured JSON tree that AI agents can walk without scraping HTML.

The same posts, pages, and taxonomies you author in WordPress flow through the @act-spec/adapter-wordpress package and land at /.well-known/act.json + /act/ on the same origin.

The rest of these posts walk through the moving parts.

title.rendered: What is an Agent Content Tree?

excerpt.rendered: A two-minute primer on the ACT spec: nodes, summaries, blocks, and how an agent walks them.

content.rendered:
An ACT tree is a graph of nodes. Every node carries a stable id, a title, a one-sentence summary, and zero or more typed content blocks (prose, data, marketing).

Agents fetch the manifest at /.well-known/act.json, then walk subtrees — each subtree shows the immediate descendants of a node as summary entries (no body) so the agent can budget before drilling in.

The body of any single node is a separate fetch. That's the whole disclosure pattern: walk summaries cheaply, fetch bodies deliberately.

excerpt.rendered: How the adapter talks to wp/v2/{posts,pages,categories,tags,users} and turns each row into one ACT node.

content.rendered:
The adapter's enumerate step pulls every collection you opt into via config.include and yields raw WordPress rows. transform then maps each row to one ACT node: titles become title, the rendered HTML becomes a prose block, and the rendered excerpt becomes the node's summary.

Categories and tags are emitted as branch nodes so the tree has structure — posts hang off their primary category. Pages keep their parent relationship intact, mirroring the WordPress page hierarchy.

excerpt.rendered: If your post excerpts are sloppy, your ACT tree is sloppy. The adapter will not invent excerpts for you.

content.rendered:
Every ACT node carries a summary field. This is the one-sentence preview an agent reads to decide whether to fetch your full post body. It's the most important text on the page.

The WordPress adapter takes the post's rendered excerpt as the summary when present. WordPress will auto-generate one from the first words of the post body if you don't author one — that produces an extracted summary, not an authored one. For best results, write deliberate excerpts in the post editor.

excerpt.rendered: Validate every emitted ACT envelope against the spec with @act-spec/validator before you publish.

content.rendered:
Adapters are easy to get 80% right. The conformance gate is how you find the last 20%.

@act-spec/validator's walkStatic function takes the manifest, index, every node envelope, and every subtree, and reports any gap against the declared conformance level. A clean run means agents will accept your tree.

This example wires the validator into conformance.ts and exits non-zero on any gap, so you can plug it straight into CI.

excerpt.rendered: A demo WordPress blog wired to ACT via @act-spec/adapter-wordpress.

content.rendered: This site is a fictional WordPress blog used to demonstrate the ACT WordPress adapter. The fixture data lives in the example repo; in a real deployment, the adapter would point at a live WP REST API.
